text: inner truth and exoteric societal demands. Being attracted to someone of the same sex is at odds with a GLB person’s environment. It could be because they live in a conservative place—but even for those who live in areas that are widely accepting of GLB, the conflicting feeling of difference from peers is often intense. Guilt accompanies this difference, and many try to change or resist their unfolding sexualities—not just in formal ways like camps that purport to turn gay teens straight, but also in private ways like prayer or dating members of the opposite sex. This resolves itself in “coming out,” which isn’t merely a moment of revealing one’s sexuality to others, but sometimes a lengthy process. It involves coming to terms with sexuality inwardly. It usually has a lot of false starts and stops, and the pain in this process—brought on by feelings of being different than peers, of concealing true identity, of becoming acutely aware of something (sexuality) that seems taken for granted by everyone else—can be nearly unbearable. But the object-body won’t be denied. Sooner or later, the object-body’s gestures become stronger than feelings of guilt and societal pressure, and in an amazing act of intention, homosexual and bisexual people affirm their identities privately and publicly, based on inner truth rather than what they are supposed or expected to feel. The body was the first indicator of truth. The body was able to give love and acceptance before the mind. 8 In the isolating and painful mythic period of separation, GLB people feel an outside-ness to their lives. When they work their way back to wholeness, they retain the memory of this separation—and it can be a great teacher of compassion. If the feelings of separation are truly healed, empathy with others in similar (but different) circumstances of isolation or pain becomes available. An inner truth of compassion shows up through its opposite: the sense of separation is what allows GLB people to experience intimacy and empathy. III No cultural phenomenon expresses our confusion about the reality of the object-body better than pornography. Indeed, pornography exposes hypocrisy and power struggles over what the object-body is, how it should be used, and who should decide both. There are parts of the object-body that we regard as having a different quality than others. If this weren’t true, what would the difference be between a sex scene in a mainstream movie and pornography? In a mainstream film, the actors really kiss, sometimes explicitly so, showing their tongues touching. They might be naked, baring breasts, asses, and sometimes even genitals. But as the camera pans down their entwined bodies, one thing is never (or at least very rarely) shown: penetration. In other words, the difference between a movie and porn is about six inches. We live in a world that is saturated in sexual suggestion, but not sex itself. The popular argument goes something like this: pornography isn’t film or art because it is really just exploitation based on “objectification” of people (usually this means women). The argument has changed to hide behind technology. A recent addition to the argument is that porn is destroying relationships. But this part of the argument rose to prominence with the rise of the internet, and these arguments against pornography are really just borrowed critiques of technology: that it creates separation and erodes real human relationships. What’s really underneath arguments against porn, once you pull away all the borrowed supplements, is still objectification. For many critics, these arguments are meant to be self-evident: objectification is bad. Porn is bad. This is easily seen in the many attacks against porn that simply state what is depicted. For example, in the hysteria around Robert Mapplethorpe’s photography, which depicted sexual acts (often featuring naked gay men), attackers would merely describe the act in the photograph. Or in Chris Hedges’s antipornography essay “The Illusion of Love,” 9 he names what he sees and hears as if it presents some sort self-evident truth: “oral sex, vaginal sex, double penetration, and double anal.” He quotes a performer who says during a shoot, “Shove it up my fucking ass,” and “Fuck, motherfucker,” and “Fucking love it.” For some reason, Hedges thinks no explanation as to why this should be problematic is required. Of course, all of this misses an important aspect of our lives: objectification isn’t something that is done to us; we are already and always part object. For those few critics of pornography who don’t believe arguments of objectification are self-evident truths, the rest of the argument goes something like, “It’s a problem because the viewer of porn sees someone only as an object.” These arguments leave out so many questions of context as to leave them impotent. Questions forgotten in this line of reasoning include: Will we react to people in life the way we do to people we watch in porn? Should we? Does all porn have the same affect, even across cultural boundaries (i.e., does straight porn exist in the heterosexual world the same way gay porn does in the gay world?)? Does porn show up in the same way in any and every cultural context? Does it change through time? Because these questions are rarely considered in antiporn arguments, most antiporn arguments are not very useful or complex. But since they are still taken seriously, we must respond to their face value. With the decline of modernistic thinking, multiplicity was revealed as a better model of sanity in psychology. The ability to move from one state to another without radical separation of those states is recognized as a sign of mental health. 10 So psychology works by allowing us to flow between places where we’ve been separated inside. 11 So in some ways it seems true that to see someone as an object without seeing anything else would be troublesome, because it creates a split in thinking.
